title: Responding to AI Distillation Without Panic
gist: A Lawfare legal analysis argues that Chinese labs' mass 'distillation' of U.S. frontier AI models isn't legally theft under copyright, patent, or trade-secret law, and that policy should instead target illegitimate access (via CFAA and information-sharing) rather than create new IP-like rights over model outputs.

themes.theme: Scale and origin of the distillation panic

insights.claim: Anthropic released a policy paper during President Trump's trip to China in May 2026 highlighting distillation attacks as a key challenge in U.S.-China AI competition, signaling that frontier labs are actively lobbying on this issue.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Frontier labs are shaping the policy narrative around distillation in ways that could favor stronger IP-style protections for their models.
insights.locator: in May, Anthropic released a policy paper during President Trump's trip to China

insights.claim: In April 2026 the White House issued an official memorandum warning of 'deliberate, industrial-scale campaigns' by Chinese entities to distill U.S. frontier AI models, and the House Foreign Affairs Committee unanimously advanced the Deterring American AI Model Theft Act.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
entities: Deterring American AI Model Theft Act
insights.so_what: Legislative momentum toward sanctions-based anti-distillation policy is already underway and could be enacted before the underlying threat is well understood.
insights.locator: In April, the White House issued an official memorandum about distillation

insights.claim: Anthropic reported in February 2026 that three Chinese AI labs generated more than 16 million exchanges with Claude through roughly 24,000 fraudulent accounts, in some cases using jailbreak prompts to extract maximal information; OpenAI and Google reported similar efforts against their own models.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of the access-abuse problem that legitimate policy should target, distinct from routine distillation.
insights.locator: In February, Anthropic reported that three Chinese AI labs had generated more than 16 million exchanges

insights.claim: Elon Musk testified in the Musk v. Altman trial that xAI had done at least some distillation of OpenAI models and acknowledged that 'generally AI companies distill other AI companies,' underscoring that distillation is standard industry practice, not unique to Chinese labs.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Undercuts framing distillation itself as inherently hostile or foreign-specific conduct, since even U.S. rivals do it to each other.
insights.quote: generally AI companies distill other AI companies
insights.locator: While on the witness stand at the recent Musk v. Altman trial

insights.claim: OSTP Director Michael Kratsios has publicly acknowledged that AI distillation, when legitimately used to produce smaller or open-weight models, is a 'vital part' of creating open models and a competitive AI ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Even the administration driving anti-distillation policy concedes the practice has legitimate, competition-enhancing uses that overbroad rules could destroy.
insights.quote: AI distillation, when legitimately used to produce… is a ‘vital part’
insights.locator: The White House has recognized this: Office of Science and Technology Policy Director Michael Kratsios

themes.theme: Why distillation doesn't fit existing IP theft law

insights.claim: Copyright law cannot support a 'model theft' claim against distillation because the copyrightable elements of a model (like source code) aren't copied, and treating AI-generated outputs themselves as copyrighted property would create a novel and legally dubious right to control downstream use of text the developer's AI, not a human, produced.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Rules out copyright as a legal basis for anti-distillation enforcement; any legislative fix claiming a copyright theory is on weak footing.
insights.locator: To start, copyright can be ruled out.

insights.claim: Patent law is a poor fit for distillation claims because distillation doesn't copy a patented implementation or practice a patented method, and frontier AI labs themselves haven't even alleged patent infringement over distillation.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Patent theory is a dead end for anti-distillation litigation or policy design.
insights.locator: Patent rights are also a poor fit.

insights.claim: Trade secret claims against ordinary distillation are weak because distillers rely only on outputs returned through a model's public-facing interface, which are available to any paying customer and therefore fail the 'reasonable efforts to keep information secret' requirement for trade secret protection.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Investors and litigators should discount trade-secret theories as a primary enforcement tool against garden-variety distillation.
insights.locator: What about trade secrets?

insights.claim: The strongest trade secret argument applies only where distillers obtain genuinely nonpublic information — full reasoning chains, agent traces, or token-level probability distributions — via coordinated proxy accounts or jailbreaks, but the author states there is no evidence Chinese distillers have obtained that class of information, only ordinary outputs any legitimate user could get.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: The legal bar for a winning trade secret case against current Chinese distillation practices is not yet met, per the author's read of the evidence.
insights.locator: The case would be different if distillers could obtain information like full nonpublic reasoning chains

insights.claim: In Compulife Software Inc. v. Newman, the Eleventh Circuit allowed a trade secret claim over mass scraping of a proprietary insurance-quote database to proceed, but that case involved copying of a proprietary database and software code — facts absent in AI distillation cases — marking the outer limit of trade secret theory that distillation doesn't reach.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Provides the closest legal precedent investors/policymakers might cite, while showing why it doesn't actually extend to distillation.
insights.locator: Compulife Software Inc. v. Newman shows the outer limits

insights.claim: Violating an AI lab's terms of service is not by itself 'theft' — if every TOS violation counted as theft the concept would have no limiting principle — so the real legal question is whether distillers use false identities or misrepresented credentials to evade access controls.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Sharpens what 'good' anti-distillation policy should actually target: access-control evasion, not TOS breach per se.
insights.quote: if every TOS violation counts as ‘theft,’ then the concept has no limiting principle
insights.locator: What's more, distillation does often violate the AI lab's terms of service

themes.theme: CFAA as the more viable legal lever

insights.claim: Under the Supreme Court's 2021 Van Buren v. United States decision, an ordinary TOS violation is generally not enough to 'exceed authorized access' under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, because the Court read the statute as focused on access restrictions to parts of a system, not on improper purpose for accessing otherwise-permitted information.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Limits how far prosecutors/labs can stretch CFAA against distillation absent actual access-control circumvention — a key constraint on enforcement strategy.
insights.locator: after the Supreme Court's 2021 decision in Van Buren v. United States

insights.claim: The CFAA remains viable where distillers use false accounts, misrepresented credentials, or proxy access to evade a lab's access cutoffs after being detected, as in United States v. Cuomo, where the Second Circuit affirmed CFAA convictions for using other people's credentials to bypass a public website's authentication gate.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific, legally defensible enforcement path against mass distillation: prosecuting credential fraud and proxy-account evasion, not distillation itself.
insights.locator: in United States v. Cuomo, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit affirmed CFAA convictions

themes.theme: Recommended policy path: access security over IP expansion

insights.claim: The author's central thesis is that anti-distillation policy should target illegitimate access — fraudulent accounts, proxy networks, and access-control evasion — rather than treat model outputs as a new form of intellectual property, because expanding IP rights in outputs would favor AI labs' commercial interests over the public's.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Sets the framework investors should use to judge whether pending AI legislation (e.g., the Deterring American AI Model Theft Act) is well-targeted or a giveaway to incumbent labs.
insights.locator: Overall, the labs' interests ought to be balanced against public interests.

insights.claim: A commonsense policy proposal is to enable information sharing and coordination among frontier AI labs and the government to identify fraudulent accounts and proxy networks, potentially via an antitrust safe harbor built on the existing cybersecurity information-sharing antitrust exemption, which has been extended through Sept. 30, 2026 pending longer-term reauthorization.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Flags a concrete near-term legislative deadline (Sept 30, 2026) and mechanism worth tracking for AI-sector antitrust/info-sharing policy.
insights.locator: which has been extended through Sept. 30, 2026, while Congress considers longer-term reauthorization

insights.claim: The author warns that defining distillation itself as trade secret theft under the Economic Espionage Act and the Protecting American Intellectual Property Act risks bleeding into private trade secret litigation and broader legislative overreach, and argues IP rights in AI models should be debated openly rather than shaped indirectly through the distillation fight.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: A contrarian warning against using national-security rhetoric to smuggle in de facto new IP rights for frontier AI labs.
insights.locator: The rhetoric around distillation as a form of IP theft

insights.claim: The author argues excessive anti-distillation restrictions could backfire by preventing legitimate open-weight models from competing, since frontier labs themselves benefit from open-source software, published research, and the commons of human knowledge — meaning one-sided restrictions would intensify concentration of AI capabilities and economic power among a few large labs.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: A second-order risk for AI-sector competitive dynamics: overbroad IP-style protection could entrench incumbents like OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic at the expense of open-model competitors.
insights.locator: AI developers have themselves benefited from open-source software and published research.

themes.theme: Unresolved question: does distillation actually transfer dangerous capability?

insights.claim: Anthropic's own data shows DeepSeek had far fewer exchanges with Claude (150,000) than Moonshot (3.4 million) or MiniMax (13 million), yet DeepSeek is among China's most powerful AI models — suggesting raw distillation volume doesn't cleanly predict resulting model capability.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Undercuts a simple 'more distillation = more capability transfer' narrative that could otherwise justify blunt volume-based restrictions; DeepSeek's efficiency is the more important signal.
insights.locator: Anthropic reported that DeepSeek had far fewer exchanges (150,000) with Claude

insights.claim: The author recommends the Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) at NIST study whether distillation materially improves dangerous capabilities, transfers or strips safety safeguards, or merely offers uplift already available from existing open-weight models, before policymakers adopt stronger restrictive measures.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: Names the specific body (CAISI/NIST) and research questions that will determine whether stronger anti-distillation law becomes justified — a concrete signpost to watch.
insights.locator: the sensible response is further investigation, potentially by the Center for AI Standards and Innovation

insights.claim: The author concludes that in the absence of clear evidence distillation meaningfully improves dangerous model capabilities, regulatory restraint is warranted to avoid policy errors driven by perceived threats that have outpaced the evidence.
insights.speaker: Bahrad A. Sokhansanj
insights.so_what: A base-rate caution for investors evaluating AI-regulation-driven risk to Chinese or open-weight AI model developers: current evidence doesn't yet support severe restriction.
insights.quote: restraint is warranted to avoid policy errors driven by perceived threats that outpaced the evidence
insights.locator: In the meantime, restraint is warranted

top_entities: Deterring American AI Model Theft Act
